```suggestion
        Flag to indicate that the connection may only execute queries and no update or DDL statements.
        If autocommit is true, the connection will use a single use read-only transaction with strong timestamp
        bound for each new statement, and will immediately see any changes that have been committed by
        any other transaction.
        If autocommit is false, the connection will automatically start a new multi use read-only transaction
        with strong timestamp bound when the first statement is executed. This read-only transaction will be
        used for all subsequent statements until either commit() or rollback() is called on the connection. The
        read-only transaction will read from a consistent snapshot of the database at the time that the
        transaction started. This means that the transaction will not see any changes that have been
        committed by other transactions since the start of the read-only transaction. Commit or rolling back
        the read-only transaction is semantically the same, and only indicates that the read-only transaction
        should end a that a new one should be started when the next statement is executed.
```

* Free-standing functions only; no classes or methods.
* No namespaces; use prefixes to avoid name clashes
* No default parameters; all parameter must be explicitly given
* No function overloading.
* Plain types only. C++ objects need to be passed as opaque handles (`void*`).

It would be acceptable to access `_parameters` directly from within this class's own methods.

```suggestion
        out._parameters = parameters
```

There's no need to reset the `identifier` and `nplike` to the values they already have. This isn't just a performance thing; I think setting only the `_parameters` would make the intent clearer for maintenance. Future readers of this code might think it's important that `identifier` and `nplike` are getting involved.

Extracting `parameters` from the property (`self.parameters` and not `self._parameters`) is important because `self._parameters` might be `None`, and if so, `self.parameters` first promotes it to `{}`, so you can be sure that what you get is a dict.

For code at this level of abstraction, it would also be acceptable to replace

```python
        parameters = copy.copy(self.parameters)
```

with

```python
        if self._parameters is None:
            parameters = {}
        else:
            parameters = copy.copy(self._parameters)
```

so that `self._parameters` doesn't get changed by looking at it, but I'm only putting in a suggested change for the first one.

There are two things that I'm waiting for: the worker queue (all the DataFrames as they are processed from each thread) and for the threads to complete. I want the worker queue to take precedence.

If the queue fills up, it can block processing because the threads will block until an item is pulled from the queue. I actually encountered this behavior when I first implemented this code, as it manifested as much slower than expected download speeds. See the following picture:

![wait for queue vs  wait for futures (1)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/247555/59523755-f9401880-8e86-11e9-9c88-06f0886b0769.png)

Notice that most of the `put` calls block because of a full queue.

One way to address the full queue is to have an inner loop to drain the queue each time. I actually tried this, too, but it resulted in bursty reads. Many `put` calls still would block.

![wait for queue vs  wait for futures (2)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/247555/59523840-2ee50180-8e87-11e9-9643-4bc40d766ccd.png)

What I've implemented instead is to not ever wait for the threads to finish, and instead block on the queue.

![wait for queue vs  wait for futures](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/247555/59523872-4623ef00-8e87-11e9-995b-40e967f0fbfe.png)

This could result in waiting longer than we need to at the end, since we could wait for a full `_PROGRESS_INTERVAL` before noticing that all streams are finished. Since these streams are expected to last quite long compared to the `_PROGRESS_INTERVAL`, I'm not as concerned about that. My main motivation is to ensure that `put` calls do not block.

I don't know what kind of caching would make sense here.

The main point is, that this action triggers only when the requirements change anyways, so 
caching of the venv by hashing the requirements files would only make sense force successive runs for the same changes in requirements (e.g. triggered by force-push).

The other caching strategy could be to cache a "global" pip-cache (cache key not depending on requirement hashes) in order to speed up download time for wheels. Downsides: probably more cache-bloat, and the wheels still have to be installed.

In general I think the advantages are marginal compared to the integration test time.

```
--merge-keys           Merge keys in successive var files not overwrite.
```

The existing behaviour is for keys found in the last `var_file` to overwrite any that were defined in earlier ones. The new behaviour when `--merge-keys` is specified is to merge these keys instead of overwriting them. It is true that in some scenarios some subkeys still end up being overwritten according to the deep merge algorithm but I can't think of any easy way to express that in a few words. I am open to suggestions.

[ RMI et RSA - Forfait Logement ]

Dans la formule de RMI-RSA [qui date de 2016](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/4bcf1e8078416b7172f43daed76379be50a13b1c), on utilise:
- pour le RMI: `rmi_fl.forfait_logement.taux1,2,3`
- pour le RSA: `rsa_fl.forfait_logement.taux_1_personne`


Hors pour le RMI, on a 2 types de paramètres:
- `rmi_fl.forfait_logement.taux1,2,3`, introduits [sur la PR de migration #798](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/99d24e6dfe7419ce17f1a2a430480574deb3a174) sans references qui valent respectivement: 1998: 0.12, 2002: 0.24 et 2002: 0.297
- `rmi_fl.forfait_logement.1_personne, 2_personnes, 3_personnes_ou_plus`, introduits [sur la PR de migration #798](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/99d24e6dfe7419ce17f1a2a430480574deb3a174) sans references qui valent respectivement: 1998: 0.12, 1988: 0.16 et 2002: 0.165, sont tous éteints en 2009 et sont ceux que l'on retrouve côté IPP


Methode: Regarder dans les parametres qui ont des references à quoi ils correspondent:
- [x] Est-ce que les valeurs sont les bonnes ? -> oui
- [x] Est-ce que ce sont bien elles qui entrent dans le calcul ? -> oui pour les 2
- [x] Qu'en est-il de la difference de dates d'application ? -> passage RMI/RSA
- [x] Est-ce qu'on trouve une trace des autres parametres ? à quoi correspondent-ils ? -> Introuvables !


La reference IPP est une super loi qui ne nous dit rien du tout: https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/loda/id/JORFTEXT000019860428/

On essaye de trouver la loi qui correspond à ce parametre en entrant par Service Public:
- https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/vosdroits/F19778

On trouve enfin les taux du FL dans la loi:

Au vu de la date, ils concernent plutot le RSA:
> 2009-06-01:
> title: Article R262-9 du Code de l'action sociale et des familles
> href: https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/codes/article_lc/LEGIARTI000020526192/2009-06-01/



Donc les taux 12,16 et 16.5, institués en 2009 seraient ceux du forfait logement RSA.

--> je supprime ceux les doublons côté RSA et je lance les tests:

- [x] Les tests passent (ils ne sont appelés nulle part)


Mais qu'en est-il des doublons 0.12, 2002: 0.24 et 2002: 0.297 ? Sont-ce ceux du RMI ? Je fouille mais je ne trouve rien. J'ai envie de les supprimer car je ne vois pas d'où ils sortent...


- [x] Je les supprime et je lance les tests: ca passe


Ils ne sortent pas de nulle part: je fais l'hypothèse que ces taux sont recalculés à partir de la loi, selon un format qui va bien dans la formule. Je fais un git blame pour retrouver les anciennes versions de la formule de calcul du RMI:

La toute premiere version de code qui utilise ces taux est ici: https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/239f09406ecba447315755e0b43a281546cc00b4 

Donc on arrive (pour la variable comme les parametres) sur une 'mega PR de migration' au-dela de laquelle on ne peut pas remonter...

Impossible de savoir d'où sortent ces valeurs de taux du forfait logement.

Finalement je fais les choix suivants:
- côté RMI et RSA on a les mêmes taux : 12, 16 et 16,5
- côté RMI ils durent de 1988 à 2009 [je n'ai trouvé la reference qu'à partir de 2004](https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/codes/article_lc/LEGIARTI000006906112/2004-10-26)
- côté RSA ils démarrent en 2009
